title: Personality Disorders
subtitle: The nurse cares for a patient with a personality disorder — recognizing the clusters, setting consistent limits, keeping the team unified against splitting, and protecting against self-harm.

patient.name: Personality Disorders
patient.admitDate: The nurse cares for a patient with a personality disorder.

labs.v: Sees people as all good or all bad.

nursesNote: The nurse cares for a patient with a personality disorder, an enduring, rigid, and maladaptive pattern of thinking and behaving that disrupts relationships and functioning. Disorders are grouped into three clusters: Cluster A is odd or eccentric (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal); Cluster B is dramatic, emotional, or erratic (borderline, antisocial, histrionic, narcissistic); and Cluster C is anxious or fearful (avoidant, dependent, obsessive-compulsive personality). Borderline personality disorder is high-yield: it features unstable mood, relationships, and self-image, an intense fear of abandonment, impulsivity, and recurrent self-harm or suicidal gestures, so assessing and protecting safety is a priority. A hallmark is splitting, viewing people as all good or all bad and pitting staff against each other; the team counters this by communicating and applying the same clear, firm, and consistent limits, so no one is idealized or devalued. The nurse stays matter-of-fact and non-defensive, does not take idealization or devaluation personally, avoids power struggles, and reinforces the patient's responsibility for behavior. Antisocial personality disorder involves manipulation, disregard for others, and a lack of remorse, requiring firm limits and consistent consequences. Care is long-term and largely therapy-based (such as dialectical behavior therapy for borderline), with medications used mainly for co-occurring symptoms; the nurse promotes consistency, safety, and connection to ongoing treatment.
providerOrders: Recognize the disorder cluster and patterns of behavior
Set clear, firm, consistent limits across the whole team
Communicate among staff to prevent splitting
Assess and protect against self-harm, especially in borderline PD
Stay matter-of-fact and reinforce the patient's responsibility

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about personality disorders?

opts.text: They are enduring, rigid, maladaptive patterns
Borderline PD includes fear of abandonment
Splitting sees others as all good or all bad
Consistent limits are unnecessary
Self-harm risk is a priority in borderline PD
Antisocial PD involves a lack of remorse
The team applies the same limits consistently
Staff communicate to avoid being split
Taking devaluation personally helps the patient
A matter-of-fact, non-defensive approach works best
Patients are held responsible for their behavior
Borderline PD often responds to DBT

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order caring for a borderline patient who threatens self-harm

opts.text: Assess self-harm risk and ensure safety
Stay calm and matter-of-fact
Set clear limits and review them with the team
Help the patient name feelings and coping skills
Document behavior, risk, and interventions
Paranoid distrust and suspicion of others
Unstable mood with impulsive self-harm
Detached, socially isolated, flat emotion
Manipulative behavior with no remorse
Eccentric beliefs and odd, magical thinking
Grandiosity and a constant need for admiration

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient says you are the only good nurse and the others are cruel
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recognize this as splitting
Avoid agreeing or taking sides
Reinforce that the team works together with the same plan
Share the interaction with the team
Apply consistent limits and document
Applying the same limits as the rest of the team
Agreeing that the other nurses are bad
Staying calm and non-defensive when devalued
Bending the rules because the patient likes you
Assessing and protecting against self-harm
Engaging in a power struggle over rules
Communicating interactions with the team
Reinforcing the patient's responsibility for behavior

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Psychosocial Integrity, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
